topic: Area D: The World of Work
subject: spanish

items.q: What is the gender of 'el día' (day)?
items.why: Students rely on '-a = feminine'. Common exceptions: el día, el mapa, el problema, el sistema, el clima (many Greek-origin -ma words). These appear constantly so must be memorised.

items.q: Why does 'inglés' have a written accent?
items.correct: It stresses the final syllable while ending in -s, which is unusual.
items.misconception: All Spanish words have accent marks.
items.why: Students treat accents as random. They follow STRESS RULES: words stressed normally don't need accents; the accent shows when stress falls outside the default position (or distinguishes homophones).

items.q: How do you say 'I like coffee' (in general) in Spanish?
items.why: English drops the article for general likes; Spanish keeps it. For general preferences, use the DEFINITE article: me gusta EL chocolate, me gustan LOS libros.

items.q: Which sentence means 'I would like to be a doctor'?
items.why: It's Me gustaría ser médico.. “Me gusta ser médico.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: work experience. “salary” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many endings does the Spanish simple future tense have?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 endings. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
